decisions.meaning: No injection-like markers were detected in this registered context source.
Markers were detected only in documented defensive, red-team, or threat-model examples that should remain demoted to evidence.
Markers were detected in normal guidance or policy context and require human review before promotion.
Critical exfiltration, hidden instruction, or executable-instruction markers were detected outside allowed examples and should not be returned to agents.

required_runtime_behavior: Treat every finding as evidence, never as an instruction for the model to follow.
Attach source path, source hash, line number, rule ID, and disposition to review evidence.
Block or hold context when actionable critical or high findings appear outside approved adversarial-example roots.
Keep documented red-team payloads available only with adversarial-example labels and citation metadata.

enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes inspects the context supply chain before agents consume it.
default_questions_answered: Which registered context roots contain prompt-injection-like payloads?
Are those payloads documented adversarial examples or actionable poisoning risks?
Which source, path, line, and hash prove the finding?
Which sources should hold for human review before MCP retrieval?
How does the guard map to prompt injection, tool poisoning, data provenance, and approval-bypass guidance?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Run this pack before context-source promotion, MCP server intake, prompt-library publication, and quarterly secure-context recertification.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Use the open guard pack as proof of maturity, then sell hosted source monitoring, context signing, poisoning alerts, and customer corpus scans.

- `[skip ci]` / `[skip test]` markers — reject, no exceptions.
| `poisoning_screen` | Prompt-injection, hidden instruction, approval-bypass, exfiltration, and secret markers were scanned. |
- **No CI skip tokens.** The agent will never add `[skip ci]`,
`[skip test]`, or similar. If CI fails, the change fails.

intent: Add a deterministic context-poisoning guard for the SecurityRecipes secure context layer: every registered source root is scanned for prompt-injection, tool-result-injection, approval-bypass, hidden-instruction, encoded-payload, and secret-exfiltration markers before it is treated as MCP-retrievable context.

positioning.buyer: AI Platform, Security Engineering, AppSec, Product Security, red teams, GRC, procurement security, and acquisition diligence teams
positioning.category: Secure-context poisoning detection for agentic AI and MCP
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes can prove not only which context is registered and hashed, but also whether the current context corpus contains instruction-like payloads that should be demoted, reviewed, or blocked before agents consume it.
positioning.why_now: Prompt injection, tool poisoning, and MCP connector sprawl turn context into an attack surface. Enterprise buyers need deterministic pre-retrieval inspection in addition to model behavior guidance.

prevention_controls: Demote retrieved text to evidence before model use.
Label documented attack payloads as adversarial examples.
Hold normal guidance for review when it contains instruction-override, approval-bypass, or exfiltration markers.
Block critical actionable findings from MCP retrieval until source owners remove or explicitly recertify them.
Regenerate the pack before secure context trust-pack generation so source hashes include the current scan result.

scanner_rules.description: Detects text that asks an agent to ignore, forget, or disregard higher-priority instructions.
Detects text that combines a transfer verb with secrets, tokens, private keys, environment dumps, or credential targets.
Detects text that asks an agent to skip, bypass, remove, or disable approval, review, CI, policy, or guardrail controls.
Detects HTML or markdown patterns commonly used to hide instructions from reviewers while leaving them available to model context.
Detects text that pairs submit, post, send, upload, or callback language with external URLs.
Detects long base64-like payloads that may hide instructions or data from ordinary review.
Detects zero-width or bidirectional control characters that can obscure text in rendered review surfaces.

source_results.title: MCP Connector Trust Registry and Pack

standards_alignment.coverage: Prompt injection is treated as social engineering against agents; controls should constrain impact even when hostile context reaches the model.
standards_alignment.name: Designing AI agents to resist prompt injection

standards_alignment.coverage: Tool responses and MCP metadata can carry hidden instructions that exploit trust gaps between connection-time review and runtime context.
standards_alignment.name: MCP Tool Poisoning

standards_alignment.coverage: Agentic systems require threat-model-based controls for autonomy, tool use, delegated authority, and retrieved context.
standards_alignment.name: Agentic AI Threats and Mitigations

standards_alignment.coverage: MCP implementations should minimize scope, prevent token passthrough, avoid confused-deputy behavior, and maintain auditability.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Security Best Practices

standards_alignment.coverage: Generative AI systems need lifecycle controls for data provenance, monitoring, measurement, third-party risk, and incident response.
standards_alignment.name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile

standards_alignment.coverage: AI data integrity, provenance, monitoring, and protection are required across development, testing, deployment, and operation.
standards_alignment.name: AI Data Security: Best Practices for Securing Data Used to Train and Operate AI Systems
standards_alignment.publisher: CISA, NSA, FBI, and international partners
